nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Oh!
 Oh, you're real. (she seems to collect herself)
 
 
 Forgive me. I haven't seen one of you up close in... it's been a while. A long while. 
 
 
 I'm Sola. I've been standing here long enough to remember when this field hummed. When everything smelled alive. 
 
 You look like you just woke up. Which is funny because something was supposed to wake up here. 
 Eventually.

choices.text: Buzz curiously - what do you mean?
Buzz uncertainly - I don't remember anything.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Before this field went quiet, before the hive near the old oak stopped making sound, something was sealed away. Carefully. I always assumed it would either work or it wouldn't.
(Sola looks at the player)
 
 
 
 Apparently it worked.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
That makes sense.
The hive near the old oak went quiet two seasons ago. But before it did, something was preserved. 
 I don't know if it was instinct or intention.
You're here. So I suppose it doesn't matter which.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
The heaviness you're feeling, that's not disorientation. Things are genuinely different from when you went under. You're not misreading the world. The world changed.
But you're in it. Which is more than I expected by now.

choices.text: Buzz - what happened to the others?
Buzz - why am I the one that woke up?

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Many things at once. 
 Over a long time. 
No single reason, which somehow makes it harder to hold.
They didn't stop coming back because they gave up. They just stopped coming back. There's a difference. I need you to know that.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text: I don't know. I've thought about it. Maybe there doesn't need to be a reason. Maybe the question worth asking isn't why you, but what now.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text: And I think you already know part of that answer. You flew straight to a flower.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
I don't know your name. 
 Bees don't really do names, do they? That's alright. I'll call you what you are.

Worker. That's not an insult, it's about the most honourable thing I know how to say. Your whole life is built around doing something that keeps the rest of us alive.
Do you actually know what that something is?

choices.text: Buzz confidently - of course I do.
Buzz curiously - tell me.
Buzz uncertainly - I'm not sure anymore.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text: Good. Then let me say it out loud anyway, for my sake. It helps me remember why I'm still trying to bloom.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text: Of course you do, somewhere deep down. But I'd like to tell you anyway. Words make things real. And this needs to feel real right now.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text: Yes. I think that's why you're here. You've been through something. You feel it, don't you? Like something important slipped out of reach. Let me put it back.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
You are a pollinator. The job sounds simple; fly from flower to flower, collect what you need. 
 But what you leave behind when you visit us... that's the whole story.
You carry pollen. Dust from one flower to another. And that transfer -that small, invisible act- is how plants reproduce. 

 Without it, they can't make seeds. Without seeds, no fruits, no nuts, no next generation.


The food that humans eat, the trees that give them shade, the roots that hold the soil together after rain; all of it traces back to what you do when you visit a flower. 
 You're not just a bee. You're a thread in something enormous.
(Sola notices something)

Do you see the riverbank from here? Past the fence, just past the old drain.


Yesterday there was nothing moving in that water. This morning I saw a fish. One fish. But one is how it starts.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text: Hey, welcome back! Looks like you've got some questions.

choices.text: Buzz softly - so what happened to us?
Look around the empty field - why is it so quiet here?

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text: (Sola's tone shifts; still gentle, but heavier)

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
It didn't happen at once. That's what I need you to understand. There was no single morning where everything changed. 

It was gradual. Like a song getting quieter, note by note, until one day you realize you can't hear it anymore.

choices.text: Buzz - what changed in the world around you?
Buzz - what changed for the bees themselves?

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
The land changed first. Fields like this one used to be wild, full of variety. Different flowers, different seasons, something always blooming. Then came the big farms.
 One crop, one chemical, one kind of everything.The pesticides were the worst part. Sprayed to protect the harvest I understand that, I do, but they didn't only reach the pests. They reached you. They changed things in ways that don't wash away with the next rain.


And the climate shifting didn't help. My blooming season moved. Your patterns didn't move with it. Timing that used to be perfect started to miss by days, then weeks.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Your colonies started shrinking. Not from one cause - from many, all at once. Parasites arrived that your ancestors had never encountered. Food became scarce - fewer flowers, fewer varieties, longer gaps between blooms.



And then there was the competition. Other species, displaced from elsewhere, moving into territories that were already struggling. 
Too many pressures. Not enough resilience left to absorb them.You were never weak. The world just kept adding weight.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
The humans intensified their farming to feed more people. I don't think most of them meant harm. 
But the side effect was this: less space for wildflowers, less food for you, more chemicals in places where chemicals were never meant to reach.
You were caught in the middle of something you didn't cause.

choices.text: Buzz - is there something even stranger going on?
Buzz - I've heard enough. I want to do something.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Yes. There is.Some hives didn't just shrink. They emptied. Not from disease you could see, not from pests you could point to. The workers simply... didn't come back.
Food still in the hive. Young bees still waiting. But the adults - the ones who flew out into the world - they never returned.

No bodies. No explanation. Just absence.
They called it Colony Collapse Disorder. A name that sounds clinical, like they understand it. They don't. Not fully. The leading theory is that something - perhaps the chemicals, perhaps the parasites, perhaps all of it together , caused the adult bees to die suddenly, out in the field, far from home.
Hives that were full in the morning, empty by evening. That's what I mean when I say this isn't one problem. This is everything, at once, with no warning.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Good. That feeling - hold onto it.
Some of them are making choices right now. Small ones.  A bottle on the ground instead of the bin. A wrapper dropped without thinking. Those small choices add up to the world I just described.
You know what to do.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Go. Fly. Do what you were made to do. And when you find a flower, stop. Listen. We've been watching this happen for a long time. We have things to tell you.
I'll be here when you come back. I'm not going anywhere.
I never could.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
HEY! Hey hey hey. 
You're the one who just stung that guy, right? 
The one who kicked the bottle into the drain? 

I'm Pip. I'm a daisy; well, barely. 
I grow in the crack between the pavement and the curb. 

People step over me every day and have absolutely no idea I'm having the best view in the whole field.

You stopped for Sola. She said you actually listened. Do you know how rare that is? Even among bees? Most of them just grab pollen and leave. Which, fair. But still.

choices.text: Buzz curiously - you know Sola?
Buzz toward the drain - what was wrong with that bottle?

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
Know her? She's been standing forty metres from me my entire life. She doesn't move much obviously, but she talks. When the wind is right I hear everything.
She told me you were different. Most things that fly through here are moving too fast to notice what's underneath them. You noticed. That matters.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
RIGHT? Thank you. I've been watching that drain for weeks. 


Every time it rains, everything people drop on this pavement goes straight through that grate and into the water system. 

Straight in. No filter. No stop.



That bottle had detergent residue in it. You could smell it from here well, I could. You can smell everything, can't you? So you definitely smelled it.
Okay. Let me back up. To understand what detergent does to our water, you need to see it yourself. Go stop the humans using it, and return when it's done.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
Okay so, detergents. You've seen the bottles. Humans use them for everything. Dishes, floors, laundry, cars; there's a detergent for every surface they own.
And look, I get it. Cleaning things is fine. Cleaning things is good, actually. The problem isn't the cleaning.

The problem is what's inside them. And what happens when that stuff doesn't stay where it's supposed to stay.

choices.text: Buzz - what's actually inside detergents?
Buzz - where does it end up?

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
Okay so it's not just soap. Soap has been around forever, it's simple. Detergents are different. They're synthetic. Built in labs to clean harder and faster.
Inside them you've got surfactants, those are the things that actually break up grease. Then bleach activators, foaming agents, fragrances, stabilizers... 
It's a long list. Each ingredient doing a specific job.


The surfactants are the tricky ones. They're really good at what they do, maybe too good. Because they don't just stop working when you're done cleaning. They keep being active. In the drain. In the pipe. In the river.



And here's the part that gets me, surfactants can only be broken down under very specific conditions. 

You need specialized microorganisms, the right environment. Most waterways don't have that. So they persist.

And when they persist near dams? You get foam. Thick, white, unnatural foam sitting on the surface of the water. I've seen it. It doesn't look like it belongs to the same world as us.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
Down the drain, through the pipes, into the rivers. That's the short version.


The longer version is, it doesn't disappear just because you can't see it anymore. The chemicals in detergents are built to persist. 

Especially the surfactants, the core cleaning agents. They only break down under very specific conditions: specialized microorganisms, the right temperature, the right environment.
Most rivers don't have that. So they accumulate.



And you start to see it. Foam on the surface of rivers near dams. Not natural foam; thick, white, sitting there like it owns the place. That foam is a symptom. Of something building up underneath.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
Here's what that foam actually does, and this is where it stops being just ugly and starts being dangerous.

Foam sits on the surface of the water. And the surface of the water is where oxygen gets in. Air to water, that's where aquatic life breathes from.
When foam covers that surface, the oxygen transfer slows. Fish, insects, everything living in that water; they start struggling for something that used to be everywhere.

choices.text: Buzz - what happens to the creatures in the water?
Buzz - does it change the water itself?

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
So first it's the oxygen. That alone starts thinning populations. But then algae starts dying and this is the part that sounds backwards until you understand it.
The algae dies, sinks, starts decomposing. Decomposition burns through oxygen. 


So now you have less oxygen from above because of the foam layer, and less oxygen in the water because decomposing algae is consuming it.

Fish. Coral. Seaweed. Aquatic insects. 
They're not dying from one dramatic thing. They're dying because the system that kept them alive is being quietly dismantled from multiple directions at once.



Biodiversity drops. Species that were already rare disappear first. Then the more common ones start to thin.

Does that sound familiar? 
A population declining not from one cause but from many, all compounding?

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
The chemicals in detergents don't just sit there. They interact. They change things.


Temperature. Salinity. Turbidity (that's how clear or murky the water is). pH levels. All of it shifts when detergents accumulate and those aren't small things. 
Every aquatic organism is calibrated to a specific range of conditions. Move one variable too far and something that thrived there can no longer survive.
It's not like the water turns visibly toxic overnight. It just becomes slightly wrong. Then more wrong. And things that used to live there start finding it harder. Then impossible.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
One more thing. And this one has a name, which makes it feel more real somehow.


Eutrophication.



It's what happens when detergents, specifically the phosphates in them, get discharged into water in high concentrations.

choices.text: Buzz curiously - what are phosphates?
Buzz - what does eutrophication actually look like?

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
Phosphates are nutrients. Plant fertilizer, basically. In the right amounts, in the right places, totally fine.

In the wrong amounts, in a closed water system? 



 They're like an invitation to a party that gets completely out of hand.


The algae in the water receives all these extra nutrients and just explodes. 
Grows faster than anything can eat it. Covers the surface. Blocks the light from reaching deeper plants. Consumes oxygen as it decomposes.
An algal bloom. That's what it's called. Green, thick, spreading. And underneath it, everything suffocating.
Fish. Seaweed. Coral. Species that relied on the plants for food, for shelter, for breeding grounds. When the plants collapse, everything that depended on them starts to collapse too.
One bottle of detergent didn't cause this. But one bottle of detergent is part of what causes this. Multiplied by millions of bottles. 
Every day.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
Picture a lake. 
Clear water, fish visible near the surface, plants growing along the edges.

Now add phosphates from detergent runoff. Consistently. For months.


The algae starts growing faster than it should. Then faster still. It covers the surface; green, thick, spreading. An algal bloom. 

It looks almost alive in the wrong way. Too much of one thing, crowding everything else out.


The algae blocks sunlight from reaching the plants below. Those plants start dying. As they decompose, they burn through oxygen. Fish struggle first. Then coral. Then everything that depended on those plants for food, shelter, breeding. The whole web starts pulling apart.


Marine plants disappearing doesn't just mean fewer plants. It means no hiding places. 
No food sources. No nurseries.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text:
So, that bottle someone dropped near a drain on a Tuesday afternoon, they probably didn't think about it for even one second.

And I just described to you a chain of events that starts with that bottle and ends with a coral reef that can't support the fish that feed the birds that... you know. It keeps going.
You can't follow every chain to its end. But you can interrupt it at the beginning. That's what you're doing.
(she looks toward the water)
There's a fish in that river today. One fish. Just sitting near the surface like it owns the place. I've been watching that water for months. That fish is new.
Go find the others. There's a flower further along the field: Venn. 


She's quieter than me. But she knows things I don't. 


Things that are still happening right underneath your feet.And hey, come back this way sometime. It gets lonely down here in the crack. The view is great but the company is limited.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
You found me. I wasn't sure you would. 
I'm not as easy to spot as the others.


I'm Venn. I've been here longer than I look. Pip told me about you, well. She said it loudly enough that I couldn't not hear.

She's not wrong about you. You stopped. You listened. That's rarer than it should be.

choices.text: Buzz gently - are you alright?
Buzz toward the cracked soil - what happened here?

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
That's a kind question. 
I'm standing. That's something.


The soil I'm rooted in has been changing for a while now. Not dramatically. Just... gradually less of what I need. Less water retention. More salt than there used to be. 
The ground gets harder to draw from every season. I've watched other daisies here stop blooming. Then stop standing. I'm still standing. But I understand them now.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
You can see it, can't you? 
The cracking.
That's not drought. 

Well, it's partly drought. But the deeper reason is that the soil stopped being able to hold water properly. Something changed in its structure. In its chemistry.
I felt it before I could have explained it. The ground beneath me started feeling different. Less like something alive and more like something tired.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
The water that reaches this soil, it carries things. Things that were never meant to stay in the ground.

Detergent components, mostly. Surfactants. Salts. They come in through irrigation runoff, through rainwater picking up residue from the pavement, through the drain Pip watches every day.
They don't announce themselves. They just accumulate.

choices.text: Buzz - what do they do to the soil?
Buzz - can you feel it happening?

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
Salts and surfactants deteriorate soil structure over time. Slowly. The kind of slowly that's easy to ignore until it isn't.

One of the things that changes is electrical conductivity. When soil is irrigated with water carrying detergent components, that conductivity rises. And rising conductivity means rising salinity.



And saline soil, overly salty soil, eventually becomes water-repellent. The ground stops absorbing rain the way it should. It can't retain moisture. Can't store it for roots to draw from.
That's what happened here. The soil didn't become toxic overnight. It just became less and less capable of doing what soil is supposed to do. Until plants like me started finding it harder to find what we need.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
Yes. Though I didn't have words for it at first.
It started as a dryness that didn't match the weather. 

Rainy weeks where I still felt parched. Because the water was landing on the surface and running off instead of being absorbed.

The soil had become water-repellent. Too much salt built up over time, from contaminated runoff, from detergent residue carried in by irrigation water. It changed the soil's structure. Its relationship with water entirely.



Rain that should have fed me just... left. Slid off the surface like it had somewhere better to be.

I felt it as thirst. But what it really was, the ground forgetting how to hold on to things.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
I don't say all of this so you'll feel hopeless. 



I say it because the ground I'm standing in is a record of every choice made near it. 
And records can be changed. 
Slowly. But changed.
There are people working on this. Not enough of them. But they exist.

choices.text: Buzz curiously - what are people doing about it?
Buzz - how would anyone even know which products are safe?

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
Some of them are changing what goes into detergents in the first place. Using organic methods. Cleaner supply chains. 

Making sure that when the product is disposed of, it leaves the smallest possible mark on soil and water.

There are certifications now. Ways of verifying it isn't just a label. Companies that monitor the entire process; production, ingredients, disposal.
ICEA and EU Ecolabel. 
If you see those on a bottle, someone has checked. Someone has verified that what's inside was made with this in mind.
It's not a complete solution. But it's a direction. And directions matter when you've been watching things go the wrong way for a long time.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
That's exactly the right question. Because most bottles don't tell you what they cost the ground.
But some do. Not through their marketing, through certification. Independent verification. 
Organizations that check the entire process: how the ingredients are sourced, how they're made, what happens when they're disposed of.

ICEA and EU Ecolabel. Two certifications that mean someone outside the company has looked at the supply chain and confirmed that the impact on soil, on water, on places like this, has been minimized as much as possible.



They're not perfect. Nothing is. But they're honest. And honesty in a supply chain is harder to find than it should be.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
There's something else in this soil. Something that's been here longer than the detergent residue. Something that will be here long after both of us are gone.
Plastic.



I want to tell you about plastic. Not because it's new information, you've seen it everywhere today. But because the numbers are the kind that need to be said out loud to feel real.

choices.text: Buzz - how much plastic are we actually talking about?
Buzz - where does it all come from?

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
Every year, roughly a quarter of all the plastic produced ends up in the environment. 


Not in a bin. Not in a recycling facility. 
In the soil. In the water. In the air.


That's approximately 80 million tons. 
Every year.


And projections suggest that by 2040, if nothing changes significantly, that number could triple.


Plastic pollution has become so embedded in the geological record that scientists have proposed it as a marker of the Anthropocene, the era defined by human impact on the planet.
We are leaving a layer in the earth's history. Made of bottles and wrappers and packaging. Future geologists will find it and know exactly when we lived.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
Everywhere. But if you want specifics: from the 1950s to 2015, humans manufactured 830 million tonnes of plastic. 
In sixty-five years.
Of that total, only about a fifth was properly disposed of, through incineration or recycling. 


Only 9% was actually recycled. The rest (79%) went to landfills. Or here. Into soil like mine. Into water like the rivers Pip described.

Plastic now makes up somewhere between sixty and eighty percent of the world's total waste. 


On European beaches alone, half of all litter consists of single-use plastic items.


Single-use. Made to be used once. Designed to last centuries in the environment. That tension, between how briefly we use something and how long it stays, is at the heart of all of this.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
The bottles you've been seeing today, the ones people drop, the ones that end up near drains and in soil, most of them held food or drink.

Plastic became the material of choice for food and beverage packaging because it works. 


It preserves quality. 
It extends shelf life. 
It keeps things safe in transit. 
The properties that make it useful are real.

choices.text: Buzz — so why is it such a problem?
Buzz — how much of this is food packaging specifically?

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
Because almost all of it is designed for a single use. You buy it. You use it once. You're done with it. But it isn't done with the world.

The same properties that make plastic durable in a supermarket make it persistent in an environment. It doesn't decompose the way organic material does. It breaks into smaller and smaller pieces (microplastics) that work their way into soil, into water, into the organisms living in both.

One million plastic bottles are purchased every minute. 
Globally. 
Every minute.
I don't say that to overwhelm you. I say it because when you see one bottle on the ground, it isn't one bottle. It's a symbol of a system that produces them faster than the world can absorb them.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
A significant portion of all plastic produced goes directly to food and beverage packaging. And nearly all of it is single-use.

One million plastic bottles purchased every minute, worldwide. Most of them, water, juice, soft drinks. Used for minutes. Designed to outlast us by centuries.
The intention wasn't harm. Plastic packaging genuinely extends shelf life. It reduces food waste in transit. 

The people who designed these systems weren't thinking about what happens to the bottle after the drink is finished.

That's the gap. Between the useful moment and the endless aftermath. Nobody planned for the aftermath. And the aftermath is what I'm growing in.

nodes.speaker: Venn
nodes.text:
You've been flying all day through a world that is measurably worse than it was. 
The soil. The water. The air above the rivers. 
The numbers are real. The changes are real.
But you're here and you stopped. And every person you interrupted today paused even for a second, and felt something break into their habit.

That interruption is small. But small things accumulate. I know that better than most.


The same way contamination builds in soil; grain by grain, season by season, awareness builds in people. 

Slowly. Then faster. Then, sometimes, it changes something.


Go back to Sola. Tell her you found me still standing. She worries.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
There you are.



You found Pip and Venn. 
I can tell, not because you look different exactly. But you fly differently. A little heavier. A little more deliberate.
That's what knowing things does to you. It adds weight. But useful weight. The kind that keeps you from drifting.

Venn said to tell you she's still standing, didn't she.


Good. That's good.

choices.text: Buzz warmly - I learned a lot out there.
Buzz seriously - it's worse than I understood.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
You did. I watched you move through the field. 
You didn't rush. You stopped at each one and actually listened.

That's not nothing. Most things that fly through here are moving too fast to notice what's underneath them. You noticed.

And now I get to tell you the part I saved. The part that connects everything you heard today to something much larger than this field.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Yes. It is and I won't pretend otherwise, you've earned honesty today.


But here's what I've learned from standing in one place for a very long time: understanding how bad something is isn't the same as having no power over it. 
It's actually the beginning of having power over it.


Let me tell you the rest. 



The part that goes beyond this field. Then you'll have the full picture. And full pictures are more useful than fragments.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Everything Pip told you about water; the foam, the oxygen, the algae, that's what happens close to the source. In rivers. In lakes. In local waterways.
But water doesn't stay local. It moves. It carries things with it. And eventually, almost everything finds its way to the same place.

The ocean.

choices.text: Buzz — how does plastic reach the ocean?
Buzz — what happens to plastic once it's in the ocean?

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
More ways than you'd think. And fewer of them dramatic.


Wastewater treatment plants filter what they can, but the smallest particles pass through. Urban runoff; rain hitting pavement, collecting residue, carrying it into drains and then waterways. 



Cargo shipping. Fishing activities. Waste from coastal communities that never reaches a proper facility.

And agricultural land. This part surprised even me. Plastic deposited in farming soils; from packaging, from equipment, from plastic mulch films, accounts for fourteen percent of all plastic released into the environment.



The amount of plastic sitting in agricultural soils right now exceeds what's been measured in marine and freshwater environments combined.
It's under the ground we grow in. In the fields that produce the food that feeds the people who drop the bottles that Pip watches roll toward the drain.

It's all connected. Underneath.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
It doesn't stay as bottles and wrappers. That's the first thing to understand.


Sunlight breaks plastic down; photodegradation, it's called. But breaking down isn't disappearing. It fragments. Into smaller and smaller pieces. Until you have particles smaller than one centimetre. Microplastics.
And microplastics are extraordinarily durable. They can persist in ocean environments for hundreds of years. Some estimates say thousands.
The ocean isn't washing plastic away. It's accumulating it. In a form too small to easily see but present in almost every sample of seawater ever tested.
And everything that lives in that water lives with those particles now. Whether it knows it or not.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text: This is the part I find hardest to hold. But I think you're ready to hold it too.

choices.text: Buzz - what happens to sea animals?
Buzz - how do animals even encounter plastic?

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Some are entangled. Larger debris (nets, rings, fragments) wrapping around bodies, restricting movement, causing injury. That's the visible kind of harm.
But the quieter harm is ingestion.
When an animal mistakes plastic for food and eats it, the plastic fills its stomach. It feels full. It stops eating. 
But it isn't nourished. It slowly starves with a full stomach.


Malnutrition. From eating something that isn't food but looks enough like food to be mistaken for it.

And beyond individual animals, areas with high plastic accumulation have become pathways for invasive species. Organisms hitchhiking on debris, arriving in ecosystems they don't belong to, displacing the ones that do.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Because plastic, in the ocean, looks like food.
Not to us. Not if we know what we're looking at. But to an animal navigating by shape and colour and movement. 
Albatrosses hunt squid. Red plastic fragments, tumbling in the water, look enough like squid that they eat them.

Sea turtles have hunted jellyfish for longer than humans have existed. A plastic bag, drifting and translucent in the current, is indistinguishable from a jellyfish to an animal whose instincts were shaped before plastic existed.


They aren't making mistakes. They're doing exactly what they evolved to do. In a world that changed around them without giving them time to adapt.
That's not their failure. That's ours.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
So now you have it. The soil beneath Venn. The water Pip watches. The ocean neither of them can see from here but both of them are connected to.

One field. One drain. One bottle rolling toward it. Traced far enough it reaches everything.


I don't tell you this so you feel small. I tell you this so you understand why what you did today matters. Why every interrupted habit matters.

You are a thread in something enormous. Now you know what the something enormous actually looks like.

choices.text: Buzz - is anything actually changing out there?
Buzz - what do I do with all of this?

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
Yes. Slowly. The way good things tend to change.
Certifications pushing manufacturers toward cleaner processes. Communities choosing differently when they understand what the choice costs. Scientists measuring what was previously invisible and measurement is the beginning of accountability.

And then there's the human I mentioned to you. Earlier today. The one who's been different lately.

I've been watching that window for three seasons. Something changed. I don't know what prompted it, but the choices coming from that direction changed.
I think something got through to them. And I think, I'm not certain, but I think that something can get through to others too. 

Given the right interruption.
The kind of interruption you've been delivering today.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
The same thing you've been doing.
Fly! 


Interrupt the habits that are still running on automatic. Come back to the flowers, there are more of us than you've found today. Each one is watching a different part of what's happening.
And there's someone I want you to find. 
A human. Near the edge of this field, the one I mentioned earlier.

Three seasons ago they started doing something different. Recycling. Planting things on their windowsill. Small choices. But I've watched small choices reshape this field over decades.
I don't know what shifted for them. But I'd like to know if something you do reaches them. Changes them further. Or finds someone like them, someone almost ready and tips them over. That's the next part of what you're here to do.

nodes.speaker: Sola
nodes.text:
When you arrived this morning I wasn't sure what to feel. Hope is a complicated thing when you've been waiting a long time. It can feel more like exposure than comfort.
But you came back. After Pip. After Venn. You came back here, to where you started. That matters more than you know.

Go find that human. Watch what they do. And keep flying, there is more of this world to interrupt than one field can show you.

The hum is coming back. I can almost hear it.
I'll be here.
